event: Paleolithic humans inhabit the region, leaving evidence at the Crvena Stijena (Red Rock) shelter near Nikšić.
Neolithic farming communities settle along the Adriatic coast and inland valleys.
Illyrian tribes, including the Docleatae and Labeatae, establish settlements across the territory of present-day Montenegro.
Greek traders operate along the Adriatic coast, influencing the local Illyrian communities.
Rome defeats the Illyrian kingdom of King Gentius, bringing the region under expanding Roman control.
Rome crushes the great Illyrian revolt and consolidates the region within the province of Illyricum.
The Roman city of Doclea (Duklja) near modern Podgorica flourishes as a regional urban center.
The division of the Roman Empire places the region within the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) sphere.
Slavic tribes migrate into the region, gradually assimilating the earlier Romanized population.
Prince Stefan Vojislav defeats a Byzantine army at the Battle of Bar, securing independence for the Slavic principality of Duklja.
Mihailo Vojislavljević of Duklja is recognized as king by Pope Gregory VII, establishing the first kingdom in the region.
Serbian Grand Prince Stefan Nemanja conquers Duklja (Zeta), incorporating it into the medieval Serbian state.
The Balšić noble family rises to power, ruling Zeta as a largely autonomous principality as the Serbian Empire declines.
The Crnojević dynasty begins consolidating control over Zeta after the end of Balšić rule.
Ivan Crnojević establishes Cetinje as the capital of Zeta and founds the monastery that becomes the seat of the Montenegrin church.
The Crnojević Printing House produces the first books printed in Cyrillic script among the South Slavs at Cetinje.
Zeta falls under Ottoman suzerainty, though the mountainous interior retains significant autonomy.
Rule passes to the prince-bishops (vladikas) of Cetinje, forming a theocratic Montenegrin polity resisting Ottoman dominance.
Danilo Petrović-Njegoš becomes vladika, founding the Petrović-Njegoš dynasty that rules Montenegro for over two centuries.
Montenegro allies with Tsarist Russia against the Ottomans, beginning a lasting Russo-Montenegrin relationship.
Montenegrins defeat the Ottomans at the Battles of Martinići and Krusi, securing de facto independence under Petar I Petrović-Njegoš.
Petar II Petrović-Njegoš publishes the epic poem The Mountain Wreath, a cornerstone of Montenegrin and South Slavic literature.
Danilo I secularizes the state, transforming Montenegro from a prince-bishopric into a secular principality.
Montenegrin forces decisively defeat the Ottomans at the Battle of Grahovac, expanding the principality's territory.
The Congress of Berlin grants Montenegro full international recognition as an independent state with enlarged borders, including the port of Bar.
Prince Nikola I grants Montenegro its first constitution and establishes a parliament (Skupština).
Montenegro is proclaimed a kingdom with Nikola I as its first king.
Montenegro joins the Balkan League and opens the First Balkan War against the Ottoman Empire.
Austro-Hungarian forces occupy Montenegro during World War I, forcing King Nikola I into exile.
The contested Podgorica Assembly deposes the Petrović-Njegoš dynasty and unites Montenegro with Serbia in the new Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes.
Montenegro becomes part of the Zeta Banovina within the renamed Kingdom of Yugoslavia.
Axis powers occupy Montenegro, and the July 13 Uprising becomes one of the first mass anti-fascist revolts in occupied Europe.
Montenegro becomes one of the six constituent republics of socialist Yugoslavia under Josip Broz Tito.
Titograd (modern Podgorica) is designated the capital of the Socialist Republic of Montenegro.
A devastating earthquake strikes the Montenegrin coast, severely damaging the historic towns of Kotor, Budva, and Bar.
Montenegrin reservists take part in the Yugoslav siege and bombardment of Dubrovnik as Yugoslavia begins to disintegrate.
Montenegro joins Serbia to form the rump Federal Republic of Yugoslavia after the other republics secede.
Montenegro is affected by the NATO bombing of Yugoslavia during the Kosovo War and adopts the German mark to distance itself from Belgrade.
Montenegro adopts the euro as its de facto currency despite not being part of the eurozone.
The Federal Republic of Yugoslavia is reconstituted as the looser State Union of Serbia and Montenegro.
Montenegrins narrowly vote for independence in a referendum, restoring sovereignty and gaining admission to the United Nations.
Montenegro adopts a new constitution defining it as a civic, democratic, and ecological state.
Montenegro formally applies for membership in the European Union.
The European Union grants Montenegro official candidate status for membership.
Montenegro opens formal accession negotiations with the European Union.
Montenegro joins NATO as its 29th member despite strong domestic and Russian opposition.
Opposition parties oust the long-dominant Democratic Party of Socialists in parliamentary elections, ending decades of single-party rule.
Jakov Milatović is elected president, defeating long-serving leader Milo Đukanović.
